topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The stage lights felt suddenly too bright, a harsh, unforgiving glare reflecting off the polished floor. Liam stood backstage, his hands clenched so tightly his knuckles were white. He’d been waiting for his set, for the familiar, raw energy of the crowd, but instead, the air was thick with a different kind of sound—a rendition of his latest track, stripped down, but utterly devoid of the soul he poured into it. When the final, lingering note faded, a palpable silence descended, broken only by the polite, scattered applause. He watched the performer bow, a slow, deliberate movement that felt like a deliberate dismissal. Liam took a shaky breath, the familiar ache in his chest tightening. He didn't move to greet the crowd; he just turned and walked toward the exit, the sound of his own footsteps echoing hollowly in the corridor.

[story 2]

Elara gripped the edge of the velvet curtain, her jaw tight enough to ache. She had been expecting a certain kind of recognition, a nod from the audience that acknowledged the years she’d poured into that melody. But what she heard instead was a clumsy, amateurish interpretation. Every strum sounded forced, every lyric sung with a vacant quality that grated on her nerves like sandpaper. When the singer finished, a ripple of polite clapping moved through the venue, a wave that seemed to wash over her, leaving her feeling utterly exposed. Elara pulled her cardigan tighter around herself, her gaze fixed on the distant exit sign. She felt a hot flush rising up her neck, a burning sensation that made her want to flee the entire space. She didn't bother with the obligatory bow; she simply turned and walked toward the shadows, needing distance from that hollow echo.

[story 3]

Marcus leaned against the brick wall, trying to appear nonchalant as the music played. He recognized the chord progression immediately—it was his. But the execution was jarring, a cheap imitation that stripped the song of its intricate layers. He watched the vocalist, their expression too earnest, too unrefined, and a cold knot formed in his stomach. When the song concluded, the ensuing applause felt thin, insincere. Marcus pushed off the wall, his movements sharp and jerky. He avoided eye contact with the audience, focusing instead on the way the other musician was packing up their gear, a gesture that felt profoundly dismissive. He walked toward the back entrance, the silence of his retreat louder than any performance could have been.
